ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Roti Plaster (roti canai with fried egg). FOODON:03302397 (flatbread food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to roti canai with egg. A00LC (Bread and similar bakery products) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 (Roti) used as closest parent; no specific code for Roti Plaster exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Roti Plaster is approximately 130 grams, comprising one piece of roti canai with a fried egg cooked on top.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Canai (with egg) — Malaysian Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Roti Plaster is roti canai with a fried egg (plaster/sunny-side up) cooked on top. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD roti canai (plain) and roti canai with egg entries. Per-100g values represent the combined dish. Where MyFCD reports roti canai with egg, those values are used directly; plain roti canai values are used as the base where the egg-topped variant is not separately listed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Flatbread and roti analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Roti Plaster found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: roti canai / Indian flatbread (white wheat flour, pan-fried with fat), which has a published GI of approximately 62 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The addition of egg does not materially alter the GI of the bread component. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 44.85) / 100 ≈ 27.8, rounded to 27.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100g; ~546 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Roti Plaster to occasional consumption, as one serving contributes approximately 546 mg sodium (~24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced salt or ghee during preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and high glycemic load per serving (~27.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Roti Plaster in small portions and pair it with a high-fibre accompaniment such as dal or vegetable curry to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the medium GI and high glycemic load per serving can cause a significant rise in blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.1 g per 100g) and cholesterol (~185 mg per 100g) from ghee and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Roti Plaster due to its saturated fat content from ghee and dietary cholesterol from the egg, and should prefer versions prepared with less ghee or a plant-based fat substitute.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roti Plaster is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean hawker breakfast and snack dish consisting of roti canai — a flaky, layered Indian-influenced flatbread made from wheat flour, ghee or margarine, water, and salt — with a fried egg (cooked sunny-side up or half-set, resembling a plaster or bandage) pressed and cooked directly on top of the roti on a hot griddle. The name 'Plaster' is a colloquial Malaysian English term referring to the egg's appearance covering the bread like an adhesive bandage (plaster). The dish is commonly served at mamak (Malaysian Indian Muslim) stalls and kopitiams across Malaysia and Singapore. Roti Plaster has a crispy, golden exterior with a soft, layered interior and a rich, savoury egg topping. It is typically eaten with dhal (lentil curry), fish curry, or sambal for dipping. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~310 kcal per 100g), meaningful protein from the egg (~9.2g per 100g), and significant carbohydrate from the wheat flour base (~34.5g per 100g). The dish has a medium glycemic index (GI ~62) and a high glycemic load per standard serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Saturated fat from ghee and cholesterol from the egg are relevant considerations for cardiovascular health. It is halal and vegetarian-suitable.
common_user_queries: What is Plaster in Malaysian food?
Is Roti Plaster the same as roti canai with egg?
How many calories in Roti Plaster?

What is the difference between Roti Telur and Roti Plaster?

confidence_reason: Roti Plaster is a well-known Malaysian hawker dish but is not listed as a discrete entry in IMR Malaysia MyFCD under this exact name. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available MyFCD entries for roti canai (plain) and roti canai with egg. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using Indian flatbread data. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact per-100g nutrient profile for Roti Plaster as a named dish.
notes: 1. The food_name in the input JSON was 'Plaster', which is the colloquial shortened name. The canonical English name is 'Roti Plaster'. The input name is preserved as the primary alias. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD roti canai with egg data; where the egg-topped variant is not separately listed, values are estimated by combining roti canai (plain) per-100g data with a proportional contribution from a fried egg, weighted to a 130g serving (approximately 100g roti + 30g egg). 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as roti canai with egg contains no significant vitamin C; this is naturally zero for this wheat-and-egg preparation. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Roti Plaster exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from IMR MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy data for comparable flatbread-with-egg preparations; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 6. Cuisine tags from input (Noodle, Dosa) appear to be misclassification artefacts from the field survey platform; Roti Plaster is a flatbread dish, not a noodle or dosa. 7. The restaurant 'New Shah Alam - Circular Road' is a Malaysian mamak-style restaurant in Singapore, consistent with serving Roti Plaster.
